caption.text: In a previous video
I told you that gamification isn't just about badges and points.
Today, I'm giving you 5 examples of gamification to apply
directly in your SaaS.
The first is the accumulated value counter
In an activity management SaaS, like Doctolib,
instead of just displaying statistics,
you concretely show what your user has gained thanks to your product.
"This month, you saved 6 hours of administrative management"
"you honored 47 appointments,
reduced your no-shows by 30%,
generating three thousand two hundred euros in revenue."
It's no longer just a statistic,
it's living proof that your
product has value and it makes your user want to do it again.
The second is the progressive unlocking of features.
For example, in a project management SaaS,
instead of giving access to everything from the start,
you let your user unlock capabilities gradually.
The more they use the product, the more they logically unlock,
like in a video game
ultimately. This creates a feeling of constant progression
and a reason to keep exploring.
The third is social completion.
For example, in an HR SaaS,
you subtly show what other users are accomplishing.
"This week,
200 recruiters like you published their first job offer."
The user no longer feels alone in their journey and has an additional reason
to move forward.
The fourth is the unexpected reward. For example, in an invoicing SaaS,
you trigger a
surprise at a moment the user didn't anticipate. Not at signup
nor after their first invoice,
but for example on their 10th invoice
a message appears "you just sent your 10th invoice,
we've unlocked an exclusive template for you"
because the unexpected creates a much stronger emotion than the predictable.
And an unlocked template is also a reason to stay.
And the fifth is invisible
status. For example, in a sales pipeline SaaS,
you give a discreet advantage to your most active users,
early access to a new feature,
priority support. Without shouting it from the rooftops,
but just a small message
"As a super active user,
you get early access." The user feels recognized,
valued, and different from others. And that goes even further than retention,
it creates loyalty.
In fact, the most powerful gamification,
is the one your user doesn't see coming,
not just a point system displayed everywhere, even if that's interesting,
but small moments that create emotion,
progression, and attachment to your product.
